g.data-narration: The Knapsack Problem is a classic NP-hard optimization problem used in resource allocation and algorithm design.
Given items with weights and values, select items to maximize value without exceeding knapsack capacity W.
g.text: Given n items with weights w[i] and values v[i], and capacity W:
Goal: Maximize Σv[i] subject to Σw[i] ≤ W

g.data-narration: There are two main variants: 0/1 Knapsack (take whole item or none) and Fractional Knapsack (can take fractions).
g.text: • Each item: take completely or not at all
• Items can be taken in fractions
• Time: O(n log n) - due to sorting

g.data-narration: The 0/1 knapsack uses a recurrence: if item fits, take maximum of including or excluding the item.
g.text: where w[i] ≤ w; otherwise dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]

g.data-narration: The DP table has dimensions (n+1)×(W+1), where dp[i][w] represents max value using first i items with capacity w.
g.text: dp[0][w] = 0 and dp[i][0] = 0 for all valid i, w (base cases)

g.data-narration: Let's solve: items with weights [2,3,4,5], values [3,4,5,6], capacity W=5. We build the DP table row by row.
g.text: We trace through items 1-4, computing dp[i][w] for w=0 to 5

g.data-narration: The DP table fills systematically. For each item, we check if including it gives better value than excluding it.
The final answer is dp[4][5]=6. Selecting item 4 alone gives maximum value within capacity 5.
g.text: Answer: Maximum Value = 6 (Select Item 4 only, weight=5)

g.data-narration: The 2D DP table can be optimized to 1D array by iterating weights in reverse order to avoid overwriting needed values.
g.text: for w from W down to 1:

g.data-narration: Fractional Knapsack uses greedy approach: sort by value-to-weight ratio (profit density) and select items greedily.
g.text: 3. Select items greedily until capacity fills
4. Take fractional part of last item if needed

g.data-narration: Example: weights [10,20,30], values [60,100,120], capacity 50. Calculate ratios and sort to maximize value.
Taking items in ratio order: full Item1 (10,60), full Item2 (20,100), partial Item3 (20/30=2/3 of 120).
Total value: 60 + 100 + 80 = 240. This greedy approach always gives optimal solution for fractional knapsack.
Key differences: 0/1 uses DP (O(nW) time), fractional uses greedy (O(n log n)). Greedy only works for fractional variant.
Greedy fails for 0/1 because taking high ratio items may block better combinations. Example: items with weights 10,20 and values 60,100, capacity 15.
g.text: Greedy: Take Item 1 → Value=60 (capacity 5 unused, can't take Item 2)
Optimal: Take nothing → Value=0 (wait, better: can't combine!) Actually max is 60

g.data-narration: Key exam tips: Identify variant first, memorize recurrence, practice DP table tracing, remember reverse iteration for space optimization.
g.text: • Always identify variant first: if items can be divided → Fractional (greedy); else → 0/1 (DP)
• Memorize: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-w[i]] + v[i]) and iterate w in reverse for 1D optimization

g.data-narration: In summary: 0/1 uses DP O(nW), fractional uses greedy O(n log n). Greedy works only for fractional due to greedy choice property.
g.text: Summary: 0/1 → Dynamic Programming | Fractional → Greedy Approach
Both exhibit optimal substructure, but only fractional has greedy choice property
